response:
* Starting each request on a new thread allows for greater flexibility in handling varying numbers of requests and can improve responsiveness by utilizing available CPU resources.  
* Using smaller batches and launching each on a separate thread can improve parallelism and reduce the risk of thread contention, especially when dealing with I/O-bound operations.  
* Thread-based parallelism can lead to better resource utilization if the web service is I/O-bound, as it allows the thread to wait for the web service to respond while other threads continue processing.  
* If the web service is CPU-bound, using smaller batches may result in higher overall throughput due to reduced contention and better utilization of CPU resources.  
* Creating batches can help manage memory usage, as each batch is processed independently and can be discarded once completed.  
* Dynamic adjustment of thread count based on external parameters can optimize performance by adapting to load changes in real-time.  
* Using a single thread for each request can be inefficient if the web service is slow or if there are many requests, as it may lead to long waiting times.  
* Thread safety must be carefully managed when processing multiple requests to avoid race conditions and ensure data consistency.  
* Both approaches have trade-offs depending on the specific characteristics of the web service and the database. *
